text: Once you're inside the pericardial space, you have to be extra careful in terms of protecting the sheath.
They published this a few years ago that once a transeptal SL3 sheath was left
alone unprotected inside the pericardial space, it actually cut one of the branches of the coronary sinus.
And what you don't want to see is this, because this is a video that Tang gave me, and this is a case where the patient had pericardial adhesions, and they were trying to break the adhesions.
We can do that if the adhesion is not too severe.
But in this case, if you are to open up the space using the ablation catheter, you should, in my experience at least, when you do that with the ablation catheter only, it's okay.
But if you use the sheet, like an Agilis or a stiffer sheet, you can actually dissect into the myocardium itself, and you can have this catastrophic complication.
The patient did well, but this is something that you try to avoid.
